content.text: Go/No-Go Decision Forum and Participants

The Go/No-Go meeting is the single point at which all pre-launch signals — technical, operational, safety, legal, and business — converge into a binary decision. Running it well requires clarity on who is in the room, what each participant assesses, how the decision is reached, and how it is recorded. Running it badly turns the forum into a rubber stamp that provides no protection against bad launches, or a deadlock engine that blocks good ones.
A Go/No-Go forum is not a status meeting. Its sole purpose is to synthesize multi-dimensional readiness signals into a decision with a clear owner. If no decision is made, the meeting failed.
This sub-subtopic covers the structure, participants, roles, authority models, agenda mechanics, decision recording, and tooling for Go/No-Go forums for Claude features and API services.

attrs.alt: Go/No-Go Forum Structure with participants and roles

content.text: Core Participants and Their Roles
The forum has a small fixed set of core participants who assess their domain and cast a vote. Adding too many participants dilutes accountability; too few creates blind spots.
Role
Participant
Domain Assessed
Authority
Launch Owner / DRI
Engineering lead or PM
Coordinates all inputs; owns the final call
Decision authority
Engineering Lead
Senior engineer or tech lead
Code freeze, test coverage, known bugs, technical risk
Voting — can block
Safety / Policy
Safety reviewer or policy DRI
Content policy compliance, safety red lines, PRR sign-off
Voting — can block
Product Manager
Feature PM
Rollout scope, user impact, comms readiness, feature flags
Voting — can block
Ops / SRE
On-call engineer or platform SRE
Infrastructure readiness, runbook live, alert thresholds, on-call coverage
Voting — can block
Legal / Compliance
Legal counsel (if required)
Regulatory concerns, terms of service updates, data handling
Advisory — called in for high-risk launches
Security
AppSec engineer (if required)
Vulnerability scan results, pen test findings
Advisory — required for launches with new attack surface
Support Lead
CX team lead
Support team briefed, docs live, escalation paths configured
Observer — can flag, cannot block
The
DRI (Directly Responsible Individual)

is always the Launch Owner. When the DRI is the same person as one of the other roles (common in small teams), they explicitly separate their two hats: first they give their domain assessment, then they synthesize all inputs and make the call. The DRI can override a single blocking vote if they accept the risk in writing, but a two-or-more-vote block is an unconditional No-Go.
Authority Model: Consensus vs. DRI-Calls
Two models are used in practice. The choice between them has significant effects on launch velocity and risk distribution.
Model 1: Single-Veto (Default for Claude API)
Any core participant can block a launch by raising a blocking concern. The DRI cannot override a single block without:
Documenting the block and the DRI's reasoning for proceeding in the launch record.
Escalating to one level above both the blocker and the DRI for a resolution within 4 hours.
If the escalation is not resolved within 4 hours, the launch is deferred.
This model is appropriate for
high-risk or externally-visible launches
: new API endpoints, changes to model behavior, safety-relevant features, or launches to the general public. The cost of a bad launch exceeds the cost of delay.
Model 2: Majority + DRI Override (For Internal or Low-Risk Launches)
The DRI can proceed over a single blocking vote if the remaining core participants have voted Go and the DRI formally accepts the risk. This model is appropriate for:
Internal-only features with no external API surface.
Infrastructure changes behind a feature flag not yet exposed to users.
Staged rollouts where the initial exposure is < 1% of traffic.

Neither model makes blocking a lightweight action. Every block must be a named concern with a clear remediation path — 'I'm not comfortable' is not a block; 'the runbook for X scenario is missing' is.
Forum Agenda and Time Budget

A Go/No-Go meeting that runs over 30 minutes indicates either a failure to complete pre-meeting preparation or a genuine problem that should have been escalated earlier. The meeting itself should not be where issues are discovered — it is where already-known issues are evaluated against go/no-go thresholds.

Go/No-Go Meeting — {service} v{version}
Time: T-0 morning, {time} UTC
Duration: 30 minutes hard stop
Attendees: Launch Owner, Eng Lead, Safety/Policy DRI, PM, Ops/SRE
           + [Legal] [Security] [Support Lead] if required

AGENDA (time-boxed)

[0:00–0:05] — Pre-read confirmation
  - Each participant confirms they read the launch doc
  - DRI confirms all pre-launch verification steps passed
  - DRI states the launch window and rollback threshold

[0:05–0:20] — Round-robin domain assessment (3 min per participant)
  Each participant states:
    1. Their vote: GO / NO-GO / CONDITIONAL GO
    2. If NO-GO: one named concern + remediation path
    3. If CONDITIONAL GO: what condition must be met before traffic shifts

  Order: Engineering → Safety/Policy → PM → Ops/SRE → optional participants

[0:20–0:25] — DRI synthesis
  - Summarize votes
  - If any NO-GO: state the block, assign remediation owner, set SLA
  - If all GO or CONDITIONAL GO: state conditions and call GO or defer

[0:25–0:30] — Next steps
  - If GO: confirm launch time, on-call, comms schedule
  - If CONDITIONAL GO: confirm gate condition and re-check time
  - If NO-GO: confirm deferral date and required actions
  - Record decision and close the meeting
Pre-Meeting Preparation Requirements

Participants who have not read the launch doc by meeting start should be recorded as abstaining — not blocking — unless they complete their review synchronously in the first 5 minutes. Pre-meeting requirements:
Participant
Pre-Meeting Requirement
Deadline
Launch Owner
Run all pre-launch verification steps; update launch doc with results
T-0 morning, 1h before meeting
Engineering Lead
Review test results, known bug list, code freeze status
T-1d
Safety / Policy
Confirm PRR sign-off; review any last-minute prompt changes
T-1d
PM
Confirm rollout config, feature flag state, comms status
T-1d
Ops / SRE
Confirm runbook live, alert thresholds set, on-call schedule
T-1d
Voting Mechanics and Recording the Decision

The vote state for each participant and the decision rationale must be recorded in the launch document, not just the outcome. This creates an audit trail that is essential for post-incident review and for justifying risk acceptance decisions.
Launch Document Decision Block

# In docs/launches/{service}-{version}.yaml
go_no_go:
  meeting_time: "2026-07-22T09:00:00Z"
  decision: "GO"                        # GO | NO-GO | DEFERRED
  decided_at: "2026-07-22T09:22:00Z"
  decided_by: "alice@example.com"       # DRI

  votes:
    engineering:
      participant: "bob@example.com"
      vote: "GO"
      notes: "All tests green; one known bug (issue #487) deferred to v2.1.1 — non-blocking"

    safety_policy:
      participant: "carol@example.com"
      vote: "GO"
      notes: "PRR signed off 2026-07-20; no changes since sign-off"

    product:
      participant: "dave@example.com"
      vote: "CONDITIONAL_GO"
      condition: "Confirm support team briefing complete before traffic shift"
      condition_met_at: "2026-07-22T09:45:00Z"
      condition_met_by: "alice@example.com"

    ops_sre:
      participant: "eve@example.com"
      vote: "GO"
      notes: "Runbook live; alerts configured; on-call coverage confirmed for 48h window"

  blocks: []        # empty = no blocks were raised

  dri_override: false          # true only if DRI overrode a single block
  dri_override_rationale: ""   # required if dri_override is true
Automated Decision Recording

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/record_go_nogo_decision.py
# Interactive CLI for recording Go/No-Go votes in the launch YAML.
import yaml, sys, json
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timezone

PARTICIPANTS = ["engineering", "safety_policy", "product", "ops_sre"]
VALID_VOTES = ["GO", "NO-GO", "CONDITIONAL_GO", "ABSTAIN"]

def prompt(q, valid=None):
    while True:
        resp = input(q).strip()
        if valid is None or resp.upper() in [v.upper() for v in valid]:
            return resp
        print(f"  Enter one of: {', '.join(valid)}")

launch_path = Path(sys.argv[1])
launch = yaml.safe_load(launch_path.read_text())
if "go_no_go" not in launch:
    launch["go_no_go"] = {}
gng = launch["go_no_go"]
gng["meeting_time"] = gng.get("meeting_time") or input("Meeting time (UTC, e.g. 2026-07-22T09:00:00Z): ").strip()
gng.setdefault("votes", {})

print(f"\nRecording Go/No-Go votes for {launch['service']} {launch['version']}")
print("=" * 50)

votes = {}
blocks = []
conditional_conditions = []

for participant in PARTICIPANTS:
    print(f"\n[{participant.upper()}]")
    email = input(f"  Participant email: ").strip()
    vote = prompt(f"  Vote (GO/NO-GO/CONDITIONAL_GO/ABSTAIN): ", VALID_VOTES).upper()
    notes = input(f"  Notes (optional): ").strip()
    entry = {"participant": email, "vote": vote, "notes": notes}

    if vote == "NO-GO":
        concern = input("  Named concern: ").strip()
        remediation = input("  Remediation path: ").strip()
        entry["concern"] = concern
        entry["remediation"] = remediation
        blocks.append({"participant": participant, "concern": concern, "remediation": remediation})

    if vote == "CONDITIONAL_GO":
        condition = input("  Condition that must be met: ").strip()
        entry["condition"] = condition
        conditional_conditions.append(condition)

    votes[participant] = entry

gng["votes"] = votes
gng["blocks"] = blocks

now = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()

if blocks:
    print(f"\n{len(blocks)} BLOCK(s) raised. Decision is NO-GO until resolved.")
    gng["decision"] = "NO-GO"
    gng["decided_at"] = now
    gng["decided_by"] = input("DRI email: ").strip()
elif conditional_conditions:
    print(f"\n{len(conditional_conditions)} conditional(s). Decision is CONDITIONAL GO.")
    gng["decision"] = "CONDITIONAL_GO"
    gng["decided_at"] = now
    gng["decided_by"] = input("DRI email: ").strip()
else:
    print("\nAll votes are GO. Decision: GO")
    gng["decision"] = "GO"
    gng["decided_at"] = now
    gng["decided_by"] = input("DRI email: ").strip()

gng["dri_override"] = False
gng["dri_override_rationale"] = ""

launch_path.write_text(yaml.dump(launch, default_flow_style=False, sort_keys=False))
print(f"\nDecision recorded in {launch_path}")
Handling No-Go Votes and Escalations
A No-Go is not a failure — it is the forum functioning correctly. The challenge is moving from No-Go to resolution without organizational friction that discourages honest blocking in future forums.
No-Go Resolution Protocol
The blocker states a named concern and a concrete remediation path (not 'I'm worried about X', but 'error rate in load test was 2.1%, must be < 1% — re-run test after this change').
The DRI assigns a remediation owner and a resolution SLA (typically 2–4 hours for T-0 blocks).
If the SLA cannot be met, the launch is deferred. The DRI updates the launch doc with the deferral reason and the new target date.
When the remediation is complete, the blocker confirms resolution. A synchronous re-check (5 minutes, not a full meeting) is sufficient.
The DRI records the resolution in the launch document and confirms the decision change to GO.
Escalation Path for Disagreements

# Escalation path for Go/No-Go disagreements
# Documented in docs/process/go-no-go-escalation.md

escalation_levels:
  level_1:
    trigger: "DRI wishes to override a single blocking vote"
    escalation_to: "Manager of both DRI and blocker"
    sla_hours: 4
    outcome: "Override approved/denied; documented in launch record"

  level_2:
    trigger: "Two or more blocking votes, or level-1 escalation unresolved"
    escalation_to: "VP of Engineering or equivalent"
    sla_hours: 24
    outcome: "Launch deferred; remediation plan required before next forum"

  level_3:
    trigger: "Safety/Policy block on any launch"
    escalation_to: "Head of Safety (non-waivable)"
    sla_hours: null   # no SLA — safety blocks are not time-pressured
    outcome: "Launch deferred indefinitely until safety concern resolved"

# Rule: Safety/Policy blocks cannot be overridden by any level of DRI escalation.
# Engineering and operational blocks can be overridden at level 1 with documented risk acceptance.
Integrating the Forum into the Deployment Pipeline

A Go/No-Go forum that exists only as a calendar invite is fragile — it can be skipped under deadline pressure. Integrating the decision into the deployment pipeline as a hard gate prevents accidental or deliberate bypasses.

# .github/workflows/deploy.yml (relevant gate step)
name: Deploy Pipeline

jobs:
  go_no_go_gate:
    name: Go/No-Go Gate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Check Go/No-Go decision recorded
        run: |
          DECISION=$(python3 scripts/read_launch_field.py             docs/launches/${{ github.event.inputs.launch_doc }}             go_no_go.decision)
          if [ "$DECISION" != "GO" ]; then
            echo "ERROR: go_no_go.decision is '$DECISION', must be 'GO'"
            echo "Record the Go/No-Go decision before deploying."
            exit 1
          fi
          echo "Go/No-Go decision: GO — proceeding to deploy"

      - name: Check decision freshness
        run: |
          # Decision must have been recorded within the last 24 hours
          python3 scripts/check_decision_freshness.py             docs/launches/${{ github.event.inputs.launch_doc }}             --max-age-hours 24

  deploy:
    name: Deploy
    needs: go_no_go_gate
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Deploy to production
        run: ./scripts/deploy.sh ${{ github.event.inputs.environment }}
Decision Freshness Check Script

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# scripts/check_decision_freshness.py
# Fails if the Go/No-Go decision is older than --max-age-hours.
import yaml, sys, argparse
from pathlib import Path
from datetime import datetime, timezone, timedelta

parser = argparse.ArgumentParser()
parser.add_argument("launch_doc")
parser.add_argument("--max-age-hours", type=int, default=24)
args = parser.parse_args()

launch = yaml.safe_load(Path(args.launch_doc).read_text())
decided_at_str = launch.get("go_no_go", {}).get("decided_at")

if not decided_at_str:
    print("ERROR: go_no_go.decided_at not found in launch doc")
    sys.exit(1)

decided_at = datetime.fromisoformat(decided_at_str.replace("Z", "+00:00"))
age = datetime.now(timezone.utc) - decided_at
max_age = timedelta(hours=args.max_age_hours)

if age > max_age:
    print(f"ERROR: Go/No-Go decision is {age.total_seconds()/3600:.1f}h old "
          f"(max allowed: {args.max_age_hours}h)")
    print("Re-run the Go/No-Go forum and update the launch document.")
    sys.exit(1)

print(f"Go/No-Go decision freshness OK: {age.total_seconds()/3600:.1f}h old "
      f"(max: {args.max_age_hours}h)")
Common Failure Patterns in Go/No-Go Forums
Failure
Symptom
Fix
Rubber-stamp forum
All votes are always GO regardless of preparation state
Require evidence links per vote; audit forums where blocks never occur
Blocking without a path
'I'm not comfortable' with no specific concern
Forum rules: blocks must name a concern + remediation; abstract discomfort is escalated, not blocking
Forum skipped under pressure
Deploy runs without recorded decision
Pipeline gate: GO decision in launch YAML is required to unlock deploy job
DRI overrides too frequently
DRI override field set to true on > 20% of launches
Manager review triggered automatically when override rate exceeds threshold
Decision made before meeting
Meeting is used to present a pre-made decision
Record meeting time in launch doc; decision timestamp must be after meeting start
Safety/Policy seat unfilled
No safety/policy vote recorded because reviewer not available
Safety/Policy vote is required for all external launches; launch defers if seat unfilled
Summary

The Go/No-Go forum is the synthesis point for all launch readiness signals. Its effectiveness depends not on the formality of the meeting but on the clarity of roles, the enforceability of the decision gate, and the discipline of honest voting.
Core participants cover engineering, safety/policy, product, and ops/SRE — each with veto authority in the single-veto model.
The DRI owns the decision but cannot override safety/policy blocks; engineering and operational blocks can be overridden with documented risk acceptance.
The agenda is 30 minutes hard stop: 5 minutes pre-read confirmation, 15 minutes round-robin domain assessment, 5 minutes synthesis, 5 minutes next steps.
Every vote — not just the outcome — is recorded in the launch YAML, providing an audit trail for post-incident review.
The deployment pipeline gates on a recorded GO decision; freshness checks prevent stale decisions from unlocking deploys.
Blocks must name a specific concern with a remediation path; abstract discomfort is not a valid block.
